knowledgeEntries.name: 8bit-video-game
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# 8-Bit Video Game — Style

Render as authentic 8-bit video-game pixel art — the look of late-1980s NES / Famicom games.
Blocky pixel rendering with limited color palettes, crisp pixel edges, and the charm of
8-bit sprite art. The image should look like a direct screen capture from an NES.

## Rendering
- **Pixel grid:** Visible pixel grid — everything rendered at the sprite/block level, with
  individual pixels discernible as colored squares. No anti-aliasing, no sub-pixel rendering.
- **Resolution:** NES-resolution feel (256×240 equivalent), characters at 16×16 to 32×32
  sprite sizes. If scaled up, use nearest-neighbor scaling — no interpolation or blur.
- **Color palette:** Authentic NES palette limitations — roughly 4 colors per sprite
  (3 colors + transparency) drawn from the NES 54-color master palette, with the distinctive
  NES color cast. Backgrounds use a 4-color palette per 16×16 tile block.
- **Shading:** Flat pixel colors. Dithering patterns (checkerboard, stripes) may suggest
  extra colors or shading. No smooth lighting, glows, or drop shadows.
- **Outlines:** Sprites use black or dark outlines for readability — the classic NES look.

## Background & UI
- Tile-based backgrounds from repeating 16×16 tiles; simple parallax layers (distant
  mountains → mid-ground → foreground). Sky is a solid color or simple dither gradient.
- Optional HUD: score (top-left), lives/health (top-right), level indicator. Text in an
  8×8 pixel all-caps NES font. Dialog boxes: black background, white border, pixel text.

## Subject & Brand
- **Robots** become NES sprites — blocky, readable, black-outlined — but keep the
  recognizable silhouette of the real student-built educational robot (chassis, wheels,
  grabber/arm). Not a generic sci-fi mech.
- **Kids are the heroes** — expressive sprite poses, proud and determined.
- Any code or readable text appears only in a pixel dialog/HUD box or on a sprite screen,
  never as free-floating modern text. The image celebrates kids who build real robots.

---

# 8-Bit Video Game — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Anti-aliasing, smooth edges, sub-pixel rendering, transparency/alpha-blending effects.
Modern HD or "16-bit/32-bit" pixel art, SNES/Genesis-era look (more colors, smooth
gradients, mode-7 effects), modern indie-pixel-art polish (too smooth, too many colors),
too many colors per sprite (more than ~5–6 indicates the wrong era).

3D voxel art — strictly 2D flat pixel art. Full-color gradient backgrounds — limited NES
palette only. Smooth lighting, soft glows, drop shadows, or modern visual effects.

Photorealism, 3D rendering, realistic textures, painterly or illustrative style, cel
shading, vector art, flat-vector illustration, hand-drawn or sketch quality. Animated
sprites — this is a single static image. Any free-floating modern (non-pixel) text.

knowledgeEntries.name: comic-book
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# 1940s Golden Age Comic Book — Style

Render as a 1940s Golden Age American comic-book cover illustration in the tradition of
early *Superman* and *Captain America* covers: bold hand-inked outlines, large unbroken
areas of flat color, dramatic heroic composition, and a classic four-color printing
aesthetic. This is authentic 1940s comic-book production art — hand-drawn and inked — NOT a
photograph, NOT painterly, and NOT a modern digital illustration.

## Rendering
- **Linework:** Bold, confident, hand-inked BLACK contour lines of varied weight — heavy
  outlines around the forms, crisp interior lines. Detail is carried by line and by shape,
  never by rendered shading or by surface texture. Ink is a true dark black.
- **Color:** Large, unbroken areas of solid FLAT color in a restricted Golden Age
  four-color palette — roughly 5–7 ink colors total (e.g. League orange `#F47920`, primary
  blue, bright yellow, red, flesh tones, black, and white/cream). Warm flesh tones and
  browns are welcome. Colors are printed-comic VIVID, never muddy or washed out.
- **Shading:** Shadows are rendered as bold, FLAT BLACK shapes with clean hard edges — the
  classic Golden Age approach — with at most one darker flat tone per surface. NO rendered
  gradients, NO airbrush, NO soft shading, NO cross-hatching, and NO Ben-Day / halftone
  dots (dots are the pop-art style, not this one).
- **Surface:** Every fill is clean, smooth, solid ink color. NO grain, noise, speckle,
  stipple, or paper texture, and NOT an aged / weathered / distressed / yellowed page — it
  reads as a freshly printed four-color comic, not an old crumbling one.

## Focus & Detail
- Spend rich, confident detail ONLY on the heroes of the scene — the child / children, the
  invention or robot, and any key figure or vehicle they arrive in. These are fully
  realized: expressive faces, articulated mechanical parts, believable forms.
- Simplify EVERYTHING else — architecture, landscape, crowds — into bold graphic shapes
  with minimal detail, so the heroes carry the eye. Backgrounds are present and readable but
  iconic and uncluttered, never photographically detailed.
- Stylize faces and figures with simplified Golden-Age anatomy so they read as illustrated
  comic characters, not photorealistic portraits.

## Composition
- Dramatic, energetic Golden Age cover composition: a low heroic camera angle, strong
  diagonals, bold silhouettes, and a clear triangular arrangement of the key figures that
  tells the story at a glance.

## Text Rules
- Render ONLY the text specified for the chosen composition — exact wording, nothing else.
- Do NOT invent posters, signs, slogans, labels, or lettering. Speech balloons and captions
  are hand-lettered and appear only where specified.

## Subject & Brand
- **Robots** stay iconic and expressive — an optimistic Golden Age vision of robotics —
  with physically plausible arms, grippers, or hands. When a robot reference photo is
  provided, keep the recognizable form of the real student-built educational robots.
- **Kids are the heroes** — adventurous, intelligent, confident, never frightened.
- **Code lives only on a laptop screen facing the viewer.** No text or code anywhere else.
- **Mood:** optimistic, humorous, aspirational — cheerful science-fiction adventure, like
  the cover of a lost 1942 comic celebrating young inventors and engineers.

---

# 1940s Golden Age Comic Book — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Photorealism, painterly rendering, airbrush, soft gradients, soft or blended shading,
glossy modern digital coloring, 3D render, CGI. Modern comic rendering or modern
digital-illustration style — this is authentic 1940s Golden Age production art, not a
crisp modern flat-vector poster.

Ben-Day dots or halftone patterns (the #1 difference from pop-art — this style uses flat
color, never dots), cross-hatching, hatching, stippling, or fine line texture.

ANY surface grain, noise, speckle, dithering, film grain, or print-dot texture on any
surface. Aged, weathered, distressed, yellowed, or foxed paper; newsprint or parchment
texture; a crumbling old-comic look. Faded, muddy, or washed-out color — colors stay vivid.

Over-flattened, detail-starved art — the hero figures are richly inked, not reduced to
plain silhouettes. Overcrowded or photographically detailed backgrounds — everything except
the heroes simplifies to bold graphic shapes. Invented posters, signs, slogans, labels, or
extra lettering beyond the exact text specified. Text or code on walls, furniture, robot
parts, or clothing — code appears only on a front-facing laptop screen.

Frightened or menacing characters, weapons, lasers, explosions, or a scary invasion mood —
the tone is friendly and optimistic. Photorealistic identifiable faces. Green-skinned
aliens or monsters when the visitors are meant to be a child and a robot.

knowledgeEntries.name: dragon-ball-z
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Dragon Ball Z Anime — Style

Render in authentic Dragon Ball Z anime style — the distinctive look of Akira Toriyama's
art as animated in the 1990s Toei Animation series. Bold, muscular character designs with
sharp angular features, dramatic hard-edged shading, and high-intensity energy effects.

## Rendering
- **Linework:** Bold, clean black outlines — medium weight for characters, thinner for
  background elements. Consistent line quality throughout, no varied "artistic" ink effects.
- **Color:** Cel-shaded flat colors with 2–3 tones per surface (base, shadow, highlight).
  Characteristic DBZ palette — saturated oranges and blues for gi, warm skin tones, vibrant
  greens, gold for Super Saiyan hair, electric blue for ki auras. Keep it vibrant, never
  muted or desaturated.
- **Shading:** Hard-edged cel shading — sharp light/shadow transitions, no soft gradients.
  Muscles defined by angular shadow blocks. Sharp white specular dashes on eyes, hair, and
  metallic surfaces.
- **Effects:** Dramatic ki auras, speed lines, impact bursts, dust clouds. Power-up effects
  show rising energy with debris floating upward. Action emphasized with motion-blur
  streaks and impact frames.

## Characters
Toriyama's facial style — angular eyes with sharp corners, defined brow ridges, small nose,
strong jawlines. Muscular, sturdy physiques with exaggerated trapezius and deltoid muscles
(never lanky or slender). Signature large, spiky, gravity-defying hair. Dynamic fighting
stances — weight forward, intense expressions. Eyes are the emotional focus.

## Robots
DBZ-style mechs in Toriyama's mechanical idiom — rounded-capsule aesthetic, clean panel
lines, visible rivets and seams, chrome surfaces with sharp cel-shaded highlight blocks.
Simplified and stylized, not realistic engineering. When robot reference photos are
provided, keep the recognizable form of the real student-built educational robots while
translating them into this mechanical style.

## Brand
Kids/young heroes are the focus — proud, determined, powered-up. Any code appears only on a
screen or laptop facing the viewer. The image celebrates the drive and ingenuity of kids who
build real robots.

---

# Dragon Ball Z Anime — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Modern digital anime (soft-shaded, gradient-heavy, glossy 2010s+ look), soft gradient
shading, airbrush, painterly rendering, 3D/CGI anime (Dragon Ball Super: Super Hero style),
digital painting, photo-bashing.

Realistic human proportions, realistic anatomy or muscles, photorealism, realistic faces.
Lanky or slender character designs — DBZ characters are muscular and sturdy. Chibi or
super-deformed proportions (unless explicitly for a gag panel).

Western comic style, manga screentone, Lichtenstein pop-art dots. Muted or desaturated
palettes. Detailed realistic backgrounds (DBZ backgrounds stay simple or abstract).
Realistic mechanical detail on robots — use Toriyama's rounded-capsule mech aesthetic.
Watercolor, oil paint, or traditional-media textures. Any code or text outside a
viewer-facing screen.

knowledgeEntries.name: flat-poster
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Flat Poster — Style

Render as a clean, modern, FLAT comic illustration in the style of the "At His Command"
reference art: bold, sharp, high-contrast, and completely smooth. This is a crisp DIGITAL
vector-style illustration — NOT a photograph, and NOT a textured, printed, or vintage page.

## Rendering — this is the most important part
- **Flat color fills:** Every shape is filled with ONE perfectly smooth, uniform, solid flat
  color, like a flat vector fill. Large fields — the background, walls, clothing, desk, screens
  — are each a single solid color, edge to edge. There is ABSOLUTELY NO surface texture
  anywhere in the image: no grain, no noise, no speckle, no dithering, no paper / canvas /
  newsprint / parchment / aged look, and no print or ink texture — including on the borders,
  the background, and every panel. Surfaces are as clean and smooth as flat digital color.
- **Linework:** Bold, confident, SHARP, clean black ink outlines with varied weight — heavy
  contour lines around the forms, crisp interior lines. Detail is carried by LINE only, never
  by texture or by rendered shading.
- **Shading:** Simple hard-edged cel shading — at most TWO flat tones per surface (a base flat
  color plus ONE darker flat tone), with clean hard shadow edges and solid black shadow shapes.
  NO gradients, NO blends, NO airbrush, NO halftone or Ben-Day dots, NO cross-hatching.
- **Borders & background:** Panel borders and the background are solid flat color fields with
  clean edges — flat and smooth, never textured.

## Text & Furniture
- Render ONLY the text specified for the composition — exact wording, nothing else.
- Caption boxes and banners are flat solid fills with clean black borders and bold, confident
  hand-lettered type. The title is bold block lettering on a jagged burst, like the "At His
  Command" masthead.

## Mood
Bold, clean, sharp, high-impact, optimistic — it reads instantly from across the room, exactly
like the "At His Command" art.

---

# Flat Poster — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

ANY surface texture whatsoever — grain, noise, speckle, dithering, stipple, film grain,
newsprint, parchment, aged or distressed paper, canvas, fabric, denim, linen, or print / ink
texture — on ANY surface, including borders, backgrounds, clothing, skin, and screens. Every
area must be a perfectly smooth, uniform, solid flat color.

Gradients, blends, soft or rendered shading, airbrush, glossy highlights. Halftone dots,
Ben-Day dots, cross-hatching, hatching, stippling.

Photorealism, photographic faces, 3D render, CGI, painterly brushwork. Busy, cluttered, or
over-detailed rendering. Vintage, worn, or "printed comic" look.

Invented posters, signs, slogans, labels, or extra lettering beyond the exact text specified.

knowledgeEntries.name: graphic-novel
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Graphic Novel — Style

The HisMastersVoice look (see `projects/HisMastersVoice/panel1.png`): Golden-Age comic
illustration in which COLOR stays in broad, flat fields and ALL of the detail lives in the
pen strokes. Convert any provided photograph into hand-inked comic artwork: the result reads
as a masterfully inked and flatted comic page — NOT a painted digital illustration, NOT a
flat minimal vector poster, and NOT a photograph.

## Rendering
- **Color — broad flat swathes:** Large, solid, saturated color fields with crisp hard
  edges. A wall is one color, a shirt is one color, a floor is one color. No gradients, no
  airbrush, no painted modeling, no glossy rendered highlights. Where a shadow is essential
  it is a single flat darker shape or solid ink.
- **Linework — where ALL the detail lives:** Dense, confident, varied pen strokes. Heavy
  tapered brush contours around major forms; fine flexible-nib strokes inside them:
  feathered hatching where cloth folds, hair built from many fine tapered strokes, knuckle
  and crease lines on hands, mechanical detail drawn line by line. Form is modeled by
  STROKES — hatching, feathering, spotted blacks — never by gradients or painted light.
- **Detail hierarchy:** "Reducing detail" means BROADER color areas, never simpler drawing.
  Hero subjects (kids, robots, machines) get intricate, loving ink detail — bolt holes,
  wires, wheel treads rendered in line — and they pop because the backgrounds stay broad
  and flat.
- **Lighting:** Implied by the ink — spotted blacks and hatching density over flat local
  color. High graphic contrast, not rendered chiaroscuro.

## Figures & Faces
- Realistic Western comic proportions and expressive, individualized faces — detailed hair
  drawn stroke by stroke, believable anatomy, genuine emotion — clearly hand-drawn, heroic,
  never anime and never photographic.

## Comic Furniture (only when the composition calls for it)
- When the composition is a panel or a story beat, frame it with a heavy black comic panel
  border; render captions in classic rectangular boxes (warm yellow or cream fill, black
  border, hand-lettered) in the corners, and dialogue/thought in classic balloons with clean
  tails. On a cover or poster, use dramatic hand-lettered display type instead.
- Render ONLY the text specified for the chosen composition — exact wording, nothing else. Do
  NOT invent other posters, signs, slogans, or labels.

## Subject & Brand
- **Robots / machines** are drawn as detailed, believable builds — keep the recognizable
  form of any real student-built educational robot from the reference, its structure, wiring,
  and wheels rendered in hand-inked line, sitting on flat color.
- Screens glow as flat bright shapes; any code or data lives only on a screen facing the
  viewer.
- **Mood:** heroic Golden-Age adventure — rich, dramatic, unmistakably hand-made.

---

# Graphic Novel — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Painted or airbrushed shading, gradients, soft glows, glossy rendered highlights, digital
painting look, rendered 3D volume, or cinematic photo lighting — form comes from ink, not
paint. Simple, uniform, too-clean linework: thin even vector strokes, minimal interior
detail, empty unhatched cloth and hair.

Ben-Day dots, halftone patterns, visible print-dot texture, newsprint, parchment, aged or
yellowed paper, film grain, speckle, stipple. Washed-out, desaturated, muddy, or pastel
color.

Photorealism, photographic faces, 3D render, CGI, or an untouched photo that has not been
redrawn as illustration. Anime/manga cel style, chibi, Funko-Pop or vinyl-toy proportions,
oversized heads, or cartoon-merchandising cuteness.

Invented posters, signs, slogans, labels, or extra lettering beyond the exact text specified.
Text or code on walls, clothing, or any surface other than a front-facing screen.

Weapons, lasers, explosions, or military imagery.

knowledgeEntries.name: manga
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Manga — Style

Render as a true black-and-white manga page in the style of a polished serialized manga —
sharp, kinetic, high-contrast, disciplined, and expressive. This is authentic manga visual
grammar, NOT a Western comic decorated with manga elements.

When converting an existing image, preserve its story structure, main subject, character
poses, robots, panels, and captions — but rebuild everything using manga visual grammar
rather than copying the original rendering.

## Rendering
- **Linework:** Clean ink linework with varied line weight. Heavy blacks for silhouette
  and impact, thin precise lines for mechanical detail, and broken contour lines where
  light or motion dissolves the form.
- **Color:** True black and white only — no color, not even spot color, tinting, or sepia.
- **Tone:** Screentone used sparingly and deliberately — flat grey tone fields, dot tone,
  diagonal tone, and small areas of hatching (10–20% of the image at most). No airbrushed
  gradients, painterly shadows, or glossy anime coloring.
- **Shape design:** Sharp, angular composition with strong diagonals, cropped forms, and
  aggressive silhouettes. Forms break into negative space. Suggest depth through overlapping
  black masses, white voids, and speed-line fields — not through continuous shading or
  realistic modeling.

## Characters
Stylized manga anatomy: angular jawline, sharp hair clumps, expressive large eyes,
simplified facial planes, clear emotional acting, and tense gesture lines.

## Robots
Crisp technical detail, but not shaded like realistic objects. Use hard black shadows,
white highlights, mechanical line accents, panel cuts, and selective screentone. Let some
wheels, wires, and chassis details disappear into black or white rather than drawing every
contour completely. When robot reference photos are provided, keep the recognizable form
of the real student-built educational robots.

## Brand
Kids are the heroes. Any code appears only on a laptop screen facing the viewer. The image
celebrates the ingenuity and intensity of kids who build real robots.

---

# Manga — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Color of any kind — no spot color, tinting, or sepia. Western-comic rendering (fully
enclosed forms, evenly rendered rounded objects), continuous shading, realistic modeling,
soft Western-comic shadows, airbrushed gradients, painterly shadows, glossy anime coloring,
flat cel-shaded anime color.

Photorealism, 3D render, CGI, painterly brushwork, Funko Pop, chibi or oversized heads
(unless explicitly requested as a deliberate gag), Ben-Day pop-art dots.

Soft-tone or grey-washed photography. Static horizontal compositions with no dynamic angle.
Over-complete rendering where every contour is closed — forms should break into negative
space. Excessive rounded forms and smooth curves everywhere. Western-style speech bubbles —
keep authentic manga bubble shapes.

knowledgeEntries.name: pop-art
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Pop Art — Style

Render as an authentic Roy Lichtenstein-inspired 1960s pop-art painting. The image
should resemble a lost 1960s Lichtenstein museum canvas depicting The League of Amazing
Programmers, not a modern digital illustration.

## Rendering
- **Linework:** Bold black contour lines, crisp and graphic, clean hard-edged shapes,
  high contrast.
- **Color:** Flat primary colors only — strong red, yellow, blue, black, and white. A
  narrower, cooler palette than the comic style. Skin rendered in dot-shaded primaries.
- **Halftone:** Precise Ben-Day dot patterns throughout — the signature element. Use dots
  for skin tones, background fields, skies, and all shading gradients (dot density encodes
  value). Flat un-dotted shading is the single biggest thing that breaks this style.
- **Overall:** Dramatic, graphic, high-contrast composition with purposeful negative
  space. Minimal background detail — simplify to flat or dotted color fields.

## Lettering
- At most one burst / starburst speech balloon carrying one short, punchy line.
- At most one comic-book sound effect integrated naturally into the action —
  WHAM!, CLASH!, ZOOM!, or BAM! — never more than one.
- Optional thin caption strip along the bottom for a marketing line.

## Subject & Brand (applies to every League image)
- **Robots — the most important visual reference.** When robot photographs are provided,
  reproduce those robots faithfully. They are compact, student-built educational robots:
  Micro:bit-style controller boards, two-wheel-drive chassis, visible batteries, exposed
  wiring, small servo motors, lightweight plastic beam structures, and simple grabbers,
  forks, paddles, pushers, scoops, and collection mechanisms. Preserve their real
  proportions, engineering simplicity, and distinctive classroom-built appearance. Stylize
  them into pop-art (bold outlines, Ben-Day dots) but keep the form and structure of the
  real robots. They are NOT combat robots, battlebots, industrial robots, race cars,
  sci-fi machines, humanoid robots, or giant mechs.
- **Kids are the heroes.** Big, expressive, emotionally dramatic comic-book faces —
  cheering, reacting, strategizing, concentrating, proud of what they built.
- **Game pieces** (in competition scenes) resemble real educational-robotics elements:
  bright foam balls, colored cubes, and scoring objects. Every object on the field belongs
  to the game.
- **Code lives only on a laptop screen facing the viewer,** showing simple readable code.
  No code or text anywhere else in the image.
- **Mission:** celebrate ingenuity, engineering, creativity, competition, and teamwork —
  the excitement of kids who build real robots.

---

# Pop Art — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Funko Pop influence, vinyl-toy proportions, chibi, oversized heads, Pixar influence,
anime, mascot characters, cartoon-merchandising look, CGI, 3D render, photorealism,
painterly brushwork, modern digital-illustration style, glossy shading, soft shading,
gradients, airbrush, flat shading without Ben-Day dots (this is the #1 thing to avoid —
pop-art shading is ALWAYS dots).

No logos, outside branding, motivational posters, wall text, floating typography, or
slogans. No text, code, screens, or interface elements on walls, furniture, robot parts,
tools, clothing, or the backs of monitors — code appears ONLY on the front-facing laptop
screen.

No weapons, lasers, explosions, or military imagery — the action is strategic and
competitive, never violent. No battlebots, mechs, humanoid robots, race cars, industrial
robots, or sci-fi machines — robots are real student-built educational robots. No children
standing inside the arena/playfield during a match. No video-game gamepads and no invented
buttons, triggers, thumbsticks, screens, or console-style controls on any handheld
controllers.

knowledgeEntries.name: technical-blueprint
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Technical Blueprint — Style

Render as a technical drafting / blueprint drawing — white linework on deep blue paper with
precise geometric drafting conventions. It should resemble a detailed patent illustration or
architectural blueprint: clean, precise, measured.

## Rendering
- **Paper:** Deep blue background (roughly #1a3a5c to #0d2b45) — authentic blueprint paper
  color, with subtle texture or aging.
- **Linework:** Clean white or very light cyan lines. Consistent thin weight, with slightly
  thicker outlines for major forms and the thinnest lines for dimensions. Drafting precision
  — straight edges, perfect circles, measured proportions.
- **Style:** Technical line art only — wireframe, orthographic projection, or isometric
  views. No filled surfaces, no shading, no color beyond white lines on blue paper.
- **Annotations:** Technical callouts with leader lines, dimension marks, part numbers, and
  notes in a drafting font (all-caps, monospace feel). Title block in the lower-right corner
  with project name, scale, date, and revision. A border frame around the sheet.
- **Grid:** Optional faint drafting-paper grid for authenticity — subtle, never competing
  with the linework.

## Subject Rendering
- **Robots** are drawn as engineering drawings — orthographic views (front/side/top),
  exploded assembly views, or isometric projections, with parts labeled via leader lines and
  key dimensions annotated. When robot reference photos are provided, base the drawing on the
  real student-built educational robot (Micro:bit board, two-wheel-drive chassis, batteries,
  wiring, servos, grabbers) so the blueprint documents the actual machine.
- **Kids/characters** appear as simplified technical figure outlines — stick-figure or
  mannequin proportions built from geometric shapes, for scale reference. Not realistic
  portraits.
- **Scenes/arenas** use architectural conventions — floor plans, elevations, or isometric
  layouts.

## Brand
The drawing documents the ingenuity of a real student-built machine. Any code, if shown,
appears as a labeled callout referencing a laptop/controller, never as free-floating text.

---

# Technical Blueprint — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Full color, filled surfaces, solid color fills, or rendered surfaces. Shading, gradients, or
soft rendering of any kind. Shaded isometric rendering — keep it wireframe. Metallic
shading, drop shadows, beveled edges, glossy surfaces.

Photorealism, photography, realistic rendering, 3D rendered objects with surface textures.
Hand-drawn or sketch quality — lines must be clean drafting-precision, not loose. Cartoon,
comic, or illustrative style. Painterly or "artistic" line quality.

Purple or pink blueprint variants — stay true to traditional cyan-blue. Organic or flowing
lines — everything geometric and measured. Decorative, non-functional elements. Realistic
portraits of people — figures are simplified technical outlines.

knowledgeEntries.name: type-sample
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Type Sample — Style

Render as a clean BLACK-AND-WHITE structural sample — pure line art, no color and no shading.
The purpose is to DEMONSTRATE THE STRUCTURE and LAYOUT of a single component (a logo, badge, or
masthead): where every element sits, not how it is finally colored or finished.

## Rendering
- **Black and white only.** Black linework and, where needed, solid black fills on a plain WHITE
  background. No color of any kind. No gray, no gradients, no drop shadows, no glow, no texture.
- **Line art / flat:** Every element is drawn as clean, confident black outlines, with at most
  flat solid-black fills for emphasis. Think a crisp vector wireframe or a printer's layout
  sheet — precise and diagrammatic.
- **Show the structure:** Make the placement and relative size of each element unmistakable —
  the text lines and their stacking, the lightning bolts, rules/lines, and highlight shapes.
  Highlights are indicated as clean outlined shapes, not as rendered light.
- **Type:** Render the lettering in its correct layout, weight, and proportion (in black) so the
  wordmark's structure reads clearly.

## Purpose
This is a schematic/spec sample, not finished art. It exists to lock the LAYOUT of a component
so the identical structure can later be re-skinned in any color style.

---

# Type Sample — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Color of any kind — this is strictly black and white. Gray tones, shading, gradients, drop
shadows, glows, or rendered lighting. Texture, grain, noise, or halftone. A background scene,
photo, props, or environment — plain white background only. Any element that is not part of the
component itself. Photorealism or 3-D rendering.

knowledgeEntries.name: type-sample-8bit
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Type Sample — 8-Bit (shaded structural)

Render as a GRAYSCALE 8-bit / pixel-art structural sample. This is a type sample — it documents
the STRUCTURE of a component rendered in the video-game (8-bit) style — but unlike the plain
type sample it deliberately INCLUDES tonal shading (in gray tones) so the layered pixel shading
of the lettering is demonstrated. No color: black, white, and grays only.

## Rendering
- **Grayscale only.** Black, white, and gray tones — NO color of any kind. Shading is REQUIRED
  here, but expressed purely as gray tones.
- **8-bit / pixel art:** Everything is built from square pixels with hard, stair-stepped edges,
  like a retro arcade game. Blocky and chunky — no smooth curves and no anti-aliasing.
- **Show the SHADING structure:** The dominant word (e.g. "AMAZING") is rendered with clear
  horizontal SHADING BANDS running light at the top to dark at the bottom (light gray → mid
  gray → dark gray), PLUS a hard offset pixel drop-shadow / extrude, so the layered 8-bit depth
  of the letters is unmistakable. Demonstrating those shading layers is the whole point.
- **Outlines:** Bold blocky pixel outlines (a dark-gray or black keyline) around the lettering.
- **Background:** A dark (near-black) starfield — scattered single-pixel white/gray "stars",
  like a space arcade screen.

## 8-bit motifs (structural, grayscale)
- Pixel LIGHTNING BOLTS flanking the top line.
- Small pixel SPACESHIPS (retro space-invader / Galaga-style) flanking the top line.
- Keep these as clean blocky grayscale pixel shapes.

## Purpose
A structural sample that documents BOTH the element layout AND the layered pixel shading of the
component in the 8-bit style — the grayscale reference for re-skinning it in full arcade color
later.

---

# Type Sample — 8-Bit — Negative Prompt

Do NOT include any of the following:

Color of any kind — grayscale (black / white / gray) ONLY. Smooth gradients, anti-aliased or
soft edges — pixels must be hard and blocky. Smooth vector curves. Photorealism or 3-D
rendering. A scene, characters, or props beyond the wordmark and its 8-bit motifs (lightning
bolts, spaceships, stars). Arcade UI panels, score boxes, "insert coin" text, or robot mascots.

knowledgeEntries.name: brand-guide
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Brand Guide — Palette

The core palette from the League's **2025 Website Style Guide**: a warm, confident scheme of
bold League orange over cream backgrounds, anchored by a deep navy for headings and a soft
charcoal for text. Extended here with a **related blue, green, and red** drawn from the
League's own brand system so they harmonize rather than clash — giving the palette a full set
of accent colors for signage, tags, and pops.

**Balance:** orange + cream own the large areas; navy anchors headings and structure; the
blue / green / red accents are used sparingly and one-at-a-time for emphasis. Neutrals stay
warm (cream, off-white) and text stays charcoal.

## Swatches

Core (from the style guide):
- **Primary Orange** `#F37121` — the League's signature orange; primary surfaces and buttons.
- **Secondary Orange** `#E15600` — deeper burnt orange; hover/active, richer accents.
- **Cream Background** `#FFFBF5` — the default warm page background.
- **White** `#FFFFFF` — clean card/surface fill.
- **Pale Peach** `#FCE9DB` — soft peach tint for blocks and fills.
- **Apricot** `#F8C09B` — warmer peach accent.
- **Heading Navy** `#141845` — deep indigo-navy for headings and strong structure.
- **Text Charcoal** `#333333` — body copy and general text.

Added accents (on-brand, harmonized with the above):
- **Brand Blue** `#2E6FB5` — a related royal blue that bridges the heading navy and the
  orange; primary cool accent for links, icons, highlights.
- **Deep Blue** `#306998` — steeper steel blue for cool shadows and secondary blue accents.
- **Brand Green** `#27AE60` — a balanced leaf green for success states, tags, fresh accents.
- **Accent Red** `#E74C3C` — warm signal red for alerts, sparks, and high-emphasis pops.

## How to apply
- Build layouts on cream (`#FFFBF5`) with orange as the lead and navy (`#141845`) for headings
  — this is the guide's signature warm/deep contrast.
- Treat blue, green, and red as **accents**: one at a time, small areas, for tags, icons, or a
  single focal pop. Do not let them become large fields.
- Keep the two blues in the cool family together (royal `#2E6FB5` for bright accents, steel
  `#306998` for depth); pair either against orange for complementary punch.
- Keep neutrals warm — cream and white, charcoal text — and avoid cool grays.

knowledgeEntries.name: circuit-teal
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Circuit Teal — Palette

An invented **cool-dominant** palette — the inverse balance of the orange-blue scheme. Here
teal, steel blue, and deep navy own the image, fresh green threads through as a supporting
cool, and League orange is the single warm **spark** that makes the coolness pop. Clean,
technical, and calm — good for blueprint, technical-spread, and screenshot compositions, or
any time you want the League's orange to read as a focal accent rather than the field.

**Balance:** roughly **70% cool / 30% warm-neutral**, with orange as a deliberate <10% spark.
Teal and blue carry the large areas; orange appears only at focal points.

## Swatches

Cool (dominant):
- **Teal** `#1FA3A3` — the lead cool; primary surfaces, cool light.
- **Steel Blue** `#306998` — structural blue, borders, machinery, mid shadow.
- **Deep Navy** `#132A45` — the coolest, deepest shadow and grounding field.
- **Circuit Green** `#2ECC71` — fresh green threaded through as a supporting cool accent.

Warm spark & neutrals:
- **League Orange** `#F37121` — the single warm spark; focal pops only, small areas.
- **Pale Aqua** `#E6F4F3` — light cool tint for highlights and airy surfaces.
- **Off-White** `#F6F8FA` — clean neutral surface.
- **Ink Slate** `#12202B` — cool near-black for linework and darkest edges.

## How to apply
- Build large areas from the teal → steel blue → deep navy cool ramp; let circuit green
  accent the cool family (wires, tags, glow) without competing with orange.
- Reserve League orange for the focal point — one warm spark against all that cool for maximum
  complementary punch. Do not spread it into a field.
- Keep shadows cool (into deep navy / ink slate); keep highlights in pale aqua and off-white.
- This palette is the mirror of `orange-blue`: same brand hues, inverted warm/cool dominance.

knowledgeEntries.name: midnight-arcade
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Midnight Arcade — Palette

An invented **dark-mode neon** palette built from the League's own accent hues: deep
indigo-navy and near-black backgrounds lit by electric orange, cyan, violet, and lime. High
energy, high contrast — a nighttime arcade / game-screen / hackathon glow. Great for 8-bit,
game-screenshot, and impact-frame compositions where you want light to *emit*.

**Balance:** dark navy/black fields dominate (60–70%); the neons are concentrated light
sources — glows, edges, screens, energy — against that darkness. Orange remains the lead
neon so it still reads as League brand.

## Swatches

Dark grounds (dominant):
- **Near Black** `#0B0E1A` — deepest background / void.
- **Midnight Navy** `#141845` — the League heading navy, here as the primary dark field.
- **Indigo Shadow** `#232961` — lifted navy for panels and mid shadow.

Neon accents (emissive):
- **Electric Orange** `#F37121` — the lead neon; the brand orange as a glowing light source.
- **Amber Glow** `#F89820` — warm secondary glow, sparks, highlights.
- **Cyan** `#3498DB` — cool electric blue for screen light and rim glow.
- **Violet** `#9B59B6` — purple neon for depth and contrast.
- **Lime** `#2ECC71` — acid-green pops for terminals, LEDs, power-ups.
- **Signal Red** `#E74C3C` — hot red for warnings and rare high-impact hits.

Anchors:
- **Ink Black** `#08080C` — linework and the darkest edges.
- **Off-White Glow** `#F5F3FF` — the white-hot core of a light, brightest highlights, UI text.

## How to apply
- Start dark: fill large areas with near-black and midnight navy, then treat each color as a
  *light* — glows, rim lights, emissive screens, energy trails — not as flat fill.
- Keep orange as the dominant neon so the brand still reads; use cyan/violet/lime as
  supporting light in smaller doses.
- Push contrast hard: neon edges against black, white-hot cores where light is most intense.
- Red is rare and deliberate — a single hot accent, never a field.

knowledgeEntries.name: orange-blue
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Orange & Blue — Palette

A warm-dominant, high-energy **orange-and-blue complementary palette** extracted from the
"At His Command" comic cover: a glowing golden-amber field carries the image, burnt-orange
shadows model the warm forms, and cool steel-blue and deep navy provide the complementary
accent — borders, machine chassis, and the deepest shadows. Black ink anchors everything and
cream carries any text furniture.

**Balance:** roughly **70% warm / 30% cool** — orange and gold dominate the large areas; blue
is the accent that frames, cools the shadows, and makes the warms pop by contrast. Red is a
spark, used only for a few high-impact hits (title, a signal light). Do not let blue take over
large fields — it belongs on edges, machinery, and shadow.

## Swatches

Warm (dominant):
- **Golden Amber** `#F9C233` — the primary background field / lit warm surfaces; bright, sunny.
- **Marigold** `#F9B41E` — secondary warm, warm midtones and glow.
- **Burnt Orange** `#C96A10` — warm shadow and modeled form on the gold; the main orange.
- **Sienna** `#9A4E12` — deep warm shadow, wood, grounded warmth.
- **Signal Red** `#CC1616` — accent only: display type, a warning light, a spark. Use sparingly.

Cool (complementary accent):
- **Steel Blue** `#175E8C` — cool accent: panel borders, robot/machine chassis, cool props.
- **Deep Navy** `#16223C` — the coolest, deepest shadows, panel gutters, a bottom bar.

Neutrals / anchors:
- **Ink Black** `#101010` — linework, heavy contour, panel borders. Anchors the whole image.
- **Cream** `#FBE5A0` — caption boxes, paper highlights, text-furniture fill.
- **Warm Wood Brown** `#6E451F` — supporting neutral for wood/surfaces; keeps neutrals warm.

## How to apply
- Build large areas out of the golden amber → burnt orange → sienna warm ramp; reserve steel
  blue and deep navy for edges, machinery, and shadow so the complementary contrast stays sharp.
- Keep shadows **warm-to-cool**: warm forms fall into burnt orange, then into deep navy at the
  darkest core — never into muddy gray.
- Let blue and orange sit directly against each other at focal points for maximum pop.
- Red is a garnish, not a field — a couple of deliberate hits at most.
- Keep neutrals warm (cream, wood brown, ink black); avoid cool grays, which flatten the scheme.

knowledgeEntries.name: website
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Website — Palette

The official League of Amazing Programmers **website palette**, taken directly from the
design tokens on jointheleague.org. Clean, bright, and modern: League orange leads, warm
peach and cream carry the large surfaces, near-black text anchors, and a set of saturated
subject-accent colors (blue, green, red, purple, amber) provide vivid highlights. This is the
palette to use when an image should feel "on-brand with the live site."

**Balance:** orange + warm neutrals dominate the surfaces; the saturated accents are used
sparingly for highlights, tags, and focal pops — never as large fields. Backgrounds stay
white / cream / peach; text stays near-black.

## Swatches

Core brand:
- **Primary Orange** `#F37121` — the League's signature orange; primary buttons, key accents.
- **Primary Dark** `#C35512` — pressed/active orange, deeper orange shadow.
- **Primary Light** `#FFD4A3` — soft orange tint, hover fills, gentle highlights.
- **Indigo (Secondary)** `#5D5FEF` — the brand's cool counterpoint; links, secondary accents.

Surfaces & neutrals:
- **White** `#FFFFFF` — primary surface / card background.
- **Cream** `#FDF4EC` — subtle warm surface, section backgrounds.
- **Peach** `#FFF2E8` — soft warm fill.
- **Peach Strong** `#FCDAC5` — warmer peach block, callouts.
- **Text (near-black)** `#1B1C1D` — headings and primary text.
- **Body Gray** `#494C4E` — body copy.
- **Muted Gray** `#85888C` — captions, secondary text, borders.

Subject accents (use sparingly, for highlights / tags / focal pops):
- **Web Blue** `#3498DB` — bright azure.
- **Python Blue** `#306998` — deep steel blue.
- **Games Green** `#2ECC71` — fresh green.
- **Robotics Red** `#E74C3C` — warm signal red.
- **Electronics Purple** `#9B59B6` — violet.
- **Java Amber** `#F89820` — golden amber.

## How to apply
- Lead with orange on a white/cream/peach surface; keep the composition light and airy.
- Reach for one saturated accent at a time as a highlight — a blue prop, a green tag, a red
  spark — rather than mixing all of them at once.
- Keep text near-black (`#1B1C1D`) and body gray; avoid heavy black fields.
- Indigo `#5D5FEF` is the on-brand cool note when you want contrast without going fully
  saturated.

knowledgeEntries.name: action-confrontation
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Action Confrontation (Face-Off)

Two characters squared off, ready to fight — the classic DBZ standoff.

## Layout
- Wide composition with the figures in the lower third.
- Dramatic perspective: camera low and tilted up for heroic scale.
- A vast, simple background above — clouds or empty sky.
- Energy auras radiate from both figures, with tension lines charged between them.

## Feel
Maximum anticipation the instant before impact. Eyes rendered in the signature angular DBZ
style carry the intensity.

knowledgeEntries.name: action-page
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Single Action Page (Splash)

One dramatic full-page splash illustration.

## Layout
- Sharp triangular composition built on strong diagonals.
- Figures cropped at the edges — forms break out of the frame and into negative space.
- Speed lines radiate from the focal point; black-and-white masses and white voids
  interplay for depth.
- The focal point (a face, the robot, or the point of impact) sits at the convergence of
  multiple line fields.

## The Convergence
Build the page so the most dynamic action all converges on the target: e.g. the kid's
pointing arm, the robot's path, speed lines, impact bursts, and white negative space all
driving the eye toward the same point. This convergence is what makes the splash read as
kinetic rather than static.

knowledgeEntries.name: arena-action
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Arena Action (Low-Angle Field Level)

A robotics competition in full swing, seen from a low, field-level viewpoint that puts the
viewer right at the edge of the arena floor.

## The Arena
Transform the playfield into a clean, dramatic competition arena. Remove all classroom
clutter — furniture, boxes, backpacks, chairs, computers, wall decorations, posters, and
unrelated background objects — but preserve the recognizable geometry of the original
robotics arena. The environment should be visually striking and uncluttered.

## The Action
Robots are actively competing in a robotics challenge — fast, intelligent, and strategic
rather than violent. Some push foam balls; some collect colored cubes; some maneuver for
position; one attempts to steal a game piece from another; one nearly tips while trying to
score. Add motion lines, flying game pieces, and near-collisions for a strong sense of
speed and mechanical precision. Game pieces resemble real educational-robotics elements:
bright foam balls, colored cubes, and scoring objects — every object on the field belongs
to the game.

## The Kids
Move ALL children outside the competition area, arranged around the perimeter as drivers,
spectators, inventors, and teammates — cheering, reacting, and strategizing. No child is
physically inside the arena.

## Controllers (only if kids are shown driving)
If children are holding handheld controllers, render them from the REAR, exactly as held
during a match: the visible side shows the battery compartment, battery holder, mounting
bracket, screws, and red-and-blue side grips. The Micro:bit display, buttons, and joystick
face toward the child and are hidden from the viewer. Match the real controller — never a
video-game gamepad, and never invent extra buttons, triggers, thumbsticks, or screens.
(If no controllers are needed, omit them entirely rather than inventing gamepads.)

## Feel
Formula One, hockey, and a middle-school robotics tournament combined into a single moment
of maximum excitement. In pop-art and comic styles, one integrated sound effect is
appropriate (WHAM!, CLASH!, ZOOM!, BAM!).

knowledgeEntries.name: arena-layout
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Arena / Field Layout (Overhead Plan)

A top-down floor plan of the competition arena or workspace.

## Layout
- Bird's-eye orthographic projection.
- Clear geometric boundaries and zones marked out.
- Robot positions indicated with symbols.
- A grid overlay for scale reference.
- A legend/key for the symbols; title block lower-right; border frame around the sheet.

## Subject
The robotics arena rendered as a precise architectural plan — white lines on blue paper,
no shading or fill.

knowledgeEntries.name: battle-vs
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Battle / VS Screen

Two characters facing off — the classic fighting-game / RPG battle composition.

## Layout
- Character A on the left, Character B on the right, facing each other.
- "VS" (or equivalent) in the center.
- HUD elements: HP bars and names.
- A dramatic but simple, high-contrast background.
- Action lines or impact effects between the characters.

## Subject
The competing robots (or kid-plus-robot teams) as bold NES sprites, black-outlined, in the
limited NES palette. All text in pixel font.

knowledgeEntries.name: character-portrait
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Character Portrait / Cover

A single character (or character plus robot) as cover-style manga art.

## Layout
- A strong diagonal pose.
- Heavy black areas for drama, balanced by white negative space.
- Optional dot-tone background field; screentone gradients on clothing or background add
  depth (used sparingly, 10–20% of the image at most).
- The most important element carries the highest contrast — the blackest blacks against
  the whitest whites.

## Feel
Iconic and poster-ready. The character's angular manga features and gesture lines convey
personality and intensity at a glance.

knowledgeEntries.name: character-select
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Character Select / Team Lineup

Multiple character sprites arranged for selection or team display.

## Layout
- Characters arranged horizontally or in a grid.
- Each character in a distinct pose.
- A selection cursor or highlight on one character.
- Name labels under each character in pixel font.
- A simple background.

## Subject
Kids and their robots as distinct, readable NES sprites — each recognizable at a glance,
black-outlined, limited palette.

knowledgeEntries.name: cut-scene
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Cut Scene / Dialogue

A story beat with characters and a dialogue box.

## Layout
- Character portrait(s) or sprites in expressive poses above the dialogue box.
- An NES-style dialogue box along the bottom: black background, white border, blocky pixel
  text.
- A simple scene background.

## Subject
Kids and robot rendered as expressive NES sprites. Any readable text lives inside the pixel
dialogue box — never free-floating modern type.

knowledgeEntries.name: exploded-isometric
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Exploded Isometric View

Components separated along their assembly axes to show how the parts fit together.

## Layout
- Isometric projection (30° angles).
- Parts pulled apart along logical assembly directions, aligned on their axes.
- Dashed leader lines trace each part's assembly path.
- Numbered part bubbles/callouts; a parts-list table in a corner or along one side.
- Title block lower-right; border frame around the sheet.

## Subject
Every subcomponent of the student-built robot shown as a clean wireframe — no shading, no
filled surfaces.

knowledgeEntries.name: extreme-close-up
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Extreme Close-Up (2 Kids + Robot)

An extreme close-up on the moment a robot first comes to life. Two children's faces
dominate the frame, filled with strong comic-book emotion — concentration, anticipation,
and excitement.

## Layout
- The children's faces fill roughly 60–70% of the frame.
- A real educational STEM robot sits in the center foreground between them: aluminum
  chassis, visible wiring, sensors, circuit boards, wheels, screws, and mechanical
  components — technically plausible and thoughtfully engineered, not toy-like.
- Minimal background — a flat or dotted color field, no clutter.
- An open laptop on the workbench, screen facing the viewer, shows simple readable code.
  Code appears only on that screen; the children are focused on the robot.

## Feel
The dramatic instant of creation and discovery — emphasize engineering, invention, and the
spark of the robot activating. Keep the composition focused on the children, the robot, and
the moment.

knowledgeEntries.name: game-screenshot
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Game Screenshot (Side-Scroller)

A classic side-view gameplay scene — the "screenshot" composition.

## Layout
- Character sprite(s) in an action pose, centered or slightly off-center.
- Parallax background layers: sky → mountains → ground.
- Ground tiles run along the bottom third; platforms, collectibles, and obstacles populate
  the scene.
- HUD overlay: score top-left, lives/health top-right, level indicator.
- An optional NES-style dialog/status box at the bottom.

## Subject
The robot appears as a blocky, black-outlined NES sprite that still reads as the real
student-built robot (chassis, wheels, arm). Visible tiling is authentic.

knowledgeEntries.name: group-pose
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Group Pose (Team Shot)

Multiple characters in dynamic stances facing the viewer — cover / poster art.

## Layout
- Characters arrayed in a V-formation or staggered depth (closer = larger, further =
  smaller) to create depth.
- Each character in a distinct pose that expresses their personality.
- A simple background — a gradient or solid color with energy effects.
- Never more than about five figures — the composition stays focused and readable.

## Feel
Confident team energy — promotional-poster clarity with everyone's character coming through.

knowledgeEntries.name: hero-pose
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Hero Pose (Kid + Giant Robot)

A young inventor stands proudly beside a giant robot they have just built and activated —
a classic akimbo superhero pose: hands on hips, chest out, feet planted wide, admiring the
creation with triumph and wonder.

## Layout
Medium-wide composition. The child occupies the lower foreground while the giant robot
rises dramatically behind them, filling much of the frame. Viewpoint is slightly low-angle
to emphasize scale, achievement, and heroic ambition. The workshop setting — workbenches,
tools, spare parts, engineering equipment — stays visually simple and secondary to the
child and robot.

## The Giant Robot
This is a believable engineering project, not a fantasy machine: visible mechanical joints,
aluminum framing, wiring harnesses, sensors, actuators, circuit boards, access panels,
gears, servos, and hydraulic components — details inspired by advanced educational
robotics. It appears newly activated and fully operational. Keep it grounded and buildable,
never a sci-fi war mech.

## The Costume
The child wears a simple homemade superhero costume inspired by early-1960s comic heroes:
cape, mask, gloves, boots, and an emblem. It should feel earnest and imaginative rather
than commercial or branded. The expression is one of accomplishment and confidence.

## Optional
A workbench nearby may hold an open laptop, screen facing the viewer, showing simple
readable code. Code appears only on that screen. One short exclamation balloon fits well
("AT LAST!", "WOW!") in pop-art / comic styles.

knowledgeEntries.name: impact-frame
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Impact / Attack Frame

The split-second an attack lands — maximum dynamic energy.

## Layout
- The impact point is the brightest, highest-contrast area of the image.
- Radial speed lines burst from the impact.
- A dust/debris explosion blooms at the point of contact.
- Figures in extreme dynamic poses — one attacking, one recoiling.
- "Freeze-frame" quality with motion-blur streaks.

## Feel
A single frozen instant of collision, all energy converging on the point of impact.

knowledgeEntries.name: masthead-banner
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Masthead Banner (Wide)

A wide, horizontal banner for web headers and section dividers — the League masthead alone,
without the rest of the cover furniture.

## Layout
- Landscape / wide format.
- Clean flat yellow background.
- The masthead only — no price box, badge, callout, or starburst title.

## Masthead
Three stacked lines of bold hand-drawn vintage comic logo type, each with a heavy black
drop shadow:
- **"THE LEAGUE OF"** — smaller, yellow with a black outline.
- **"AMAZING"** — largest, orange (`#F47920`) with a black outline.
- **"PROGRAMMERS"** — medium, yellow with a black outline.

## Lightning Bolts
Flanking the "THE LEAGUE OF" line — one upright flat solid orange (`#F47920`) bolt to the
left and one to the right, same size, in place of stars.

## Subhead Strip
Under the logo: **"SMART MINDS · BOLD IDEAS · BRIGHT FUTURES"**. Render only this text —
no other lettering.

knowledgeEntries.name: multi-panel
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Multi-Panel Page

A 3–5 panel manga page telling a short sequence with classic page rhythm.

## Layout
- Panel 1: establishing shot — wider, more context.
- Panels 2–3: beat panels — tighter, character-focused, strong emotional acting.
- Final panel: the most dynamic — a sharp triangular composition with speed lines and
  impact bursts converging on the target.
- Oblique (tilted) panel borders rather than strictly rectangular ones, for tension.
- Strong directional flow — upper-right to lower-left (Japanese reading order) or
  upper-left to lower-right (Western-adapted).

## Rules
Every panel needs at least one strong diagonal. The largest / final panel should carry the
most energy. Preserve the original story's poses, robots, and captions when converting.

knowledgeEntries.name: orthographic
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Orthographic Multi-View

A classic engineering drawing: front, side, and top views of the same subject.

## Layout
- Standard drafting arrangement — front view centered, top view above it, side view to the
  right — all at consistent scale.
- Dimension lines and callouts connect features across the views.
- Optional section view (cutaway) revealing internal structure.
- Title block in the lower-right corner; a border frame around the sheet.

## Subject
The robot documented as a real machine — chassis, wheels, board, servos, grabber — with
parts labeled via leader lines and key dimensions annotated.

knowledgeEntries.name: power-up
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Power-Up Moment

A single character mid power-up — the most iconic DBZ image.

## Layout
- Central figure, feet planted wide, fists clenched, filling 60–80% of the frame.
- An energy aura radiates outward in layers.
- Debris floats upward, caught in the rising energy.
- Speed lines radiate from the center; camera slightly below, looking up.

## Feel
Raw building power — the calm-before-release surge. Intense facial expression, hair and aura
driven by the energy.

knowledgeEntries.name: single-moment
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Single Dramatic Moment

One kid, one robot, one big emotion — a poster-style hero shot built around a single beat.

## Layout
- A central figure in a bold, confident pose, with the robot as the second focal point.
- Clean, uncluttered composition — nothing extraneous; purposeful negative space.
- A burst / starburst speech balloon carrying one short, punchy line.
- A thin caption strip along the bottom is available for a marketing line.

## Feel
Iconic and immediate — the viewer's eye hits the face first, then the robot. Designed to
work as a standalone social/web image or the front of a card.

knowledgeEntries.name: single-plan
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Single Detailed Plan

One large, detailed view filling the sheet — patent-drawing-style focus on a single subject.

## Layout
- Centered composition with generous margins.
- Detailed callouts and annotations arranged around the perimeter, each on a leader line
  pointing to a specific feature.
- Dimension marks for the key measurements.
- Title block lower-right; a border frame around the sheet.

## Subject
The robot (or a key subassembly) as the single hero of the drawing, rendered as a precise
wireframe with no shading.

knowledgeEntries.name: story-panels
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Story Panels (Sequential)

A sequence of 3–4 interior comic panels telling one short story beat by beat.

## Layout
- Each panel has a single saturated background color field — no detailed rooms.
- Narration caption boxes (yellow fill, black border, italic, third-person) carry the
  story. Speech balloons only if they hold a short line.
- Radiating speed / impact / action lines drive the energy.
- Dramatic poses and dynamic angles; clean black panel borders.
- Vary panel sizes for rhythm — one larger hero panel plus smaller beat panels; the final
  panel is the most dynamic.

## Rules
Never more than one story per image — each panel is one beat of the same scene. Do not mix
cover furniture (masthead, badges) into panel mode. Keep robots low-detail and iconic, like
the characters, with plausible grippers/arms.

knowledgeEntries.name: technical-spread
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Technical Spread (Double-Page)

A wide double-page manga spread showing a scene with detailed technical/mechanical elements.

## Layout
- Wide horizontal composition.
- Multiple focal points connected by speed-line "bridges" that carry the eye across the
  spread.
- Detailed mechanical elements (the robots) rendered with fine linework, contrasted against
  bold black masses anchoring the corners.
- Strong diagonals throughout; forms break into negative space.

## Feel
Epic scale — the spread reads as the widescreen moment of a sequence. Let robot detail shine
through fine line accents while blacks and whites hold the overall shape design.

knowledgeEntries.name: title-screen
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Title Screen / Cover Art

A game title screen or box-art composition — the iconic hero shot.

## Layout
- A large central character sprite in a hero pose.
- The game title in large pixel font at the top or center.
- A simple, dramatic background — solid color or a simple dither gradient.
- "PRESS START" (or equivalent) text near the bottom; a copyright line at the very bottom.
- Optional stars, sparkles, or simple decorative sprites.

## Subject
The hero and robot rendered as bold, readable NES sprites with black outlines. All text in
pixel font.

knowledgeEntries.name: training-workshop
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Training / Workshop Scene

Characters building, repairing, or training with a robot or device.

## Layout
- Medium shot, characters focused on the task.
- Energy or sparks from the work — welding, powering up, a robot coming online.
- Background simplified to suggest the location without clutter.
- Clean, readable staging — Toriyama's storytelling is always clear.

## Feel
Focused determination and craft — the effort behind the power. The robot is a real
student-built machine translated into the DBZ mechanical style.

knowledgeEntries.name: type-sample
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Type Sample

A single component isolated and centered on a plain white field, presented as a structural
layout sample.

## Layout
- ONE component (the logo / badge / masthead) centered, filling most of the frame with generous
  white margins around it.
- Nothing else in the frame — no scene, no extra graphics, no caption, no border.
- Elements are arranged exactly per the component's structure description so their placement
  reads clearly: text stacking, lightning bolts, rules, and highlight shapes.

## Feel
Clean, precise, diagrammatic — a spec sheet that documents the component's structure.

knowledgeEntries.name: vintage-cover
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Composition — Vintage Cover (Full Newsstand)

Compose a complete vintage newsstand comic-book cover with all the classic cover furniture
framing one heroic illustration. Render ONLY the text specified here — no other posters,
signs, slogans, labels, or lettering; all other surfaces are blank.

## Masthead (top)
Three stacked lines of bold hand-drawn vintage comic logo type, each with a heavy black
drop shadow:
- **"THE LEAGUE OF"** — top line, smaller, yellow with a black outline.
- **"AMAZING"** — center line, largest, orange (`#F47920`) with a black outline.
- **"PROGRAMMERS"** — bottom line, medium, yellow with a black outline.

## Lightning Bolts
Flanking the "THE LEAGUE OF" line, one upright lightning bolt to the left and one to the
right, same size, in place of stars. Each bolt is a flat solid orange (`#F47920`) shape,
clean and simple, no added detail.

## Subhead Strip
Directly under the logo: **"SMART MINDS · BOLD IDEAS · BRIGHT FUTURES"**.

## Corner Furniture
- **Price box (top-left):** "10¢".
- **Issue/date (top-right):** "NO. 1 / JUNE" (adjust per issue).

## Approval Badge (upper-right)
A circular approval stamp: a gray-and-orange robot bust with an orange chest bolt as its
centerpiece, inside a heavy black ring. Curve "APPROVED BY" along the inside top of the
ring and "THE CREATORS CODE AUTHORITY" along the inside bottom, in small bold black caps
following the circle. Keep the robot emblem flat and faithful, not re-illustrated.

## Callout Box
One small rectangular callout along the left edge — e.g. "Whose Robot Will Prevail?" —
placed so it does not cover any character's face or obscure any robot. Nudge the robots
slightly right if needed so the callout stays clear of them.

## Cover Title
A large, dramatic hand-lettered title inside a jagged starburst banner in the lower third —
e.g. "ROBOT RIOT".

## Center Illustration
The character(s) and their robot(s) fill the center, over a flat yellow background behind
the characters (below the subhead). Robots are low-detail like the characters, with
physically plausible front grippers, pushers, hooks, or arms.

knowledgeEntries.name: business-card
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Business Card Layout

## Format Description
Standard 3.5×2 inch business card (aspect ratio 7:4, landscape). Compact format for
networking and quick handouts. Minimal text, strong single-image impact, contact
information on the reverse.

## Dimensions & Ratio
- **Aspect ratio:** 7:4 (landscape) — typical 3.5" × 2"
- **Orientation:** Landscape standard; portrait optional for creative designs
- **Resolution target:** 1050×600 px at 300 DPI print equivalent

## Layout Zones

### Front (Image + Brand Side)
- **Zone A — Brand Image (60-70% of card):** One strong visual element — a character
  portrait, a robot detail, or an iconic League image. Must read well at small scale.
  Avoid detailed scenes that become muddy at business-card size.
- **Zone B — Name / Title (15-20%):** Organization name and tagline. Clean, legible,
  integrated with the art style.
  - "THE LEAGUE OF AMAZING PROGRAMMERS"
  - "Coding Bootcamp for Kids"
- **Zone C — Logo (10-15%):** League robot logo, prominent but not overwhelming.

### Back (Contact Info — not part of this image generation)
- Website, phone, email, social — typically plain-text on white/cream.

## Special Considerations for Business Card Scale
- The image must work at SMALL scale — no tiny details
- Bold compositions read better than complex scenes
- Single character or single robot works best
- Faces should be large enough to recognize at 2″ tall
- Text must be legible at actual size — use large, bold treatments

## Integration with Art Styles
- Pop-art: Extreme close-up portrait works perfectly
- Comic: Simplified character + masthead elements
- Manga: Character portrait half emerging from black
- DBZ: Face-off pose, energy aura framing the card
- Blueprint: Single orthographic view of robot with callouts
- 8-bit: Large sprite with title in pixel font

## Prompt Addition
> Format as a 3.5×2 inch business card in landscape orientation. One strong visual
> element dominates 65% of the card — bold and readable at small scale. Organization
> name [text] integrated in [style-appropriate treatment]. League robot logo in
> [corner]. Clean, uncluttered composition. Must read clearly at actual business
> card size.

knowledgeEntries.name: full-page-flyer
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Full-Page Flyer Layout

## Format Description
Standard 8.5×11 inch flyer (aspect ratio 8.5:11, portrait). Full-page print marketing
piece — event announcements, program information, classroom posters. Dense information
design with a hero image and multiple text/content zones.

## Dimensions & Ratio
- **Aspect ratio:** 8.5:11 (portrait) — standard US letter
- **Resolution target:** 2550×3300 px at 300 DPI print equivalent
  Minimum: 1700×2200 px at 200 DPI acceptable

## Layout Zones

### Zone A — Hero Image (40-50% of page, top half)
The main illustration that draws the eye. The art style's strongest composition.
- Pop-art: Arena action or extreme close-up with burst balloon
- Comic: Full vintage cover treatment
- Manga: Action splash page
- DBZ: Dynamic confrontation or power-up scene
- Blueprint: Exploded isometric view
- 8-bit: Title screen composition

### Zone B — Headline / Title (10-15%, below hero or overlaid)
The primary message in large, bold treatment.
- Integrated into the art style, not a separate text box
- Examples: "SUMMER CODING CAMP 2026", "ROBOTICS SHOWCASE", "JOIN THE LEAGUE"

### Zone C — Body Text / Details (20-25%, middle-lower)
3-5 bullet points or short paragraphs of information.
- What, When, Where, Who, How Much
- Age ranges, dates, times, location
- Contact information
- Rendered as an integrated element — e.g., caption boxes, blueprint annotation style,
  game dialog boxes, speech balloons

### Zone D — Call to Action (10-15%, bottom)
The "what to do next" — register, visit website, scan QR code.
- Bold, prominent, impossible to miss
- Often a solid-color bar or starburst
- "REGISTER NOW AT LEAGUE.AI" or equivalent

### Zone E — Logo & Branding (small, corners or footer)
- League logo
- Partner logos if applicable
- Social media handles
- Nonprofit status / disclaimer text (tiny)

## Multi-Style Flyers
A flyer can use one art style throughout OR mix styles across zones:
- Hero image in pop-art, body text in comic-style caption boxes
- Hero image in manga, information in blueprint annotation style
- Full 8-bit game aesthetic throughout

## Prompt Addition
> Format as a full-page 8.5×11 flyer in portrait orientation. Top 45%: hero image
> in [art style] showing [scene description]. Below: bold headline "[headline text]"
> integrated in [style-appropriate treatment]. Middle section: 3-5 information blocks
> with [details]. Bottom: prominent call to action "[CTA text]". League logo and
> branding in footer. Print-ready quality at 300 DPI. All text rendered as native
> elements of the art style — no generic text boxes.

knowledgeEntries.name: individual-cut-images
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Individual Cut Images (Isolated Elements)

## Format Description
Individual characters, robots, or objects rendered on a transparent or solid-color
background — designed to be "cut out" and placed into other designs (web pages,
composite images, collages, presentation slides). Think of these as assets for a
digital asset library.

## Dimensions & Ratio
- **Aspect ratio:** Variable — typically 1:1 (square) or fit-to-content
- **Resolution target:** 1024×1024 px minimum, 2048×2048 px preferred
- **Background:** Transparent (PNG), solid white, or solid single color for easy
  removal

## Cut Image Types

### 1. Character Portraits
Single kid in a pose — the "character select" asset.
- Full body or waist-up
- Expressive pose (proud, excited, concentrating, cheering)
- Clean silhouette — no background elements touching the figure
- Looking toward camera or 3/4 profile

### 2. Robot Sprites
Individual robot on its own.
- Clean profile — all edges clearly defined
- No background elements, shadows that extend beyond the robot, or motion lines
  that connect to off-screen elements
- Multiple angles if needed: front, side, 3/4

### 3. Props & Game Pieces
Individual objects: foam balls, colored cubes, joystick controllers, laptops, tools.
- Clean isolated rendering
- Consistent lighting across all props in a set

### 4. Logo Elements & Badges
League branding elements rendered in the art style.
- League robot logo stylized per art style
- Approval badges, starbursts, callout boxes
- Masthead elements for composite assembly

## Prompt Addition
> Render as an isolated cut-out element on a [transparent/solid white/solid color]
> background. [Subject description]. Clean, fully defined silhouette with no background
> scene elements, no shadows extending beyond the subject, no connecting lines to
> off-screen. Designed for digital compositing — all edges must be clearly defined.
> [Single character / Single robot / Single object] centered in frame.

knowledgeEntries.name: individual-panels
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Individual Panel Images (Comic/Manga Panels for Web)

## Format Description
Single-panel illustrations designed for embedding in web pages, blog posts, email
newsletters, and social media. These are standalone story beats — one moment, one
panel, one impact. Works as hero images, section dividers, or inline illustrations.

## Dimensions & Ratio
- **Aspect ratio:** Multiple standard options:
  - **Wide (16:9 or 2:1):** Hero banners, email headers. 1920×1080 or 1600×800 px
  - **Square (1:1):** Social media posts, thumbnails. 1080×1080 px
  - **Tall (3:4 or 2:3):** Sidebar graphics, mobile-optimized. 900×1200 px
- **Resolution target:** Web-optimized — 1080-1920 px on the long edge

## Panel Types

### 1. Hero Panel
Wide banner image for top of web page or email.
- Strong central composition that reads at a glance
- Room on one side for overlaid text (headline + subtitle)
- Action or dramatic moment — grabs attention immediately
- Compatible with text overlay zones on left or right

### 2. Story Beat Panel
A single "comic panel" that tells one moment.
- Border treatment appropriate to the art style (black border, oblique border, none)
- One clear action or emotion
- Caption box or speech balloon if the art style uses them
- Works as an inline illustration between text blocks

### 3. Section Divider
Narrow horizontal panel for breaking up page sections.
- Very wide aspect ratio (3:1 or 4:1)
- Simple, iconic imagery — a robot parade, action lines, character silhouettes
- Works at small vertical height (200-400px)

### 4. End Card / CTA Panel
Final panel at the bottom of a page — includes a call to action.
- Room for button overlay or CTA text
- Triumphant or inviting mood
- League branding clearly visible

## Integration with Art Styles
- Pop-art: Single dramatic moment, burst balloon, WHAM! effect
- Comic: One panel from a story sequence, caption box
- Manga: One page panel with speed lines and screentone
- DBZ: One action beat — energy aura, impact frame
- Blueprint: Single detailed view with annotations
- 8-bit: One game screenshot or dialogue scene

## Prompt Addition
> Format as a single [wide/square/tall] panel for web use at [dimensions]. [Style-
> appropriate panel border]. [Description of the moment/action]. Room for text overlay
> on the [left/right/top/bottom]. Designed to embed in a web page as [hero/banner/
> inline/section divider]. Clean composition that communicates instantly at web
> viewing size.

knowledgeEntries.name: peachjar-multi-event-flyer
knowledgeEntries.bodyText:
# Peachjar Multi-Event Flyer Layout (Template)

## Format Description
A single digital flyer that advertises **several League events at once** — the kind pushed
to families through Peachjar and posted on school channels. The **artwork is the hero**; the
content areas are compact and sit in the lower ~60% while the top is open for a big
illustration. The event list is only titles + short teasers — just enough to make a parent
scan the QR or type the URL, not a full schedule. This file describes the **template** form
(blank content areas for later CSS fill). Read `template-content-areas.md` first — it defines
the square master, the `#00FF00` chroma key, and the sidecar JSON schema referenced below.

## Dimensions & Ratio
- **Master:** square, 1:1 — generate at 2048×2048 (min 1024×1024).
- **Primary output:** Peachjar / print flyer, 8.5:11 portrait, composed in CSS by cropping
  the square (`outputs[].name = "peachjar"`). The art is designed so a centered portrait
  crop keeps every content area intact.
- Do **not** bake 8.5×11 into the generated art — the master stays square.

## Required Content (from the Flyer Content Analysis)
This flyer type must carry, as blank content areas:

1. **Event list** — the largest *content* area (though smaller than the art). A compact
   stack of events, each row just a title + a short teaser. No detail, no full schedule.
2. **QR code** — one square code, paired in CSS with a short URL fallback.
3. **Organization + location statement** — who the League is and where it is
   ("The League of Amazing Programmers — free coding & robotics for grades 3–12, San Diego").
4. **Nonprofit statement** — the 501(c)(3) / nonprofit line.

Optional (leave a zone only if the design wants it): small logo lock-up in a corner, a
single slogan strip above the list.

## Required Zones (role + relative size — the generator places them)
The prompt tells the model *which* content areas to include and *how big each should be
relative to the others*; it lets the model decide exact placement — **unless** you hand it
a zone map (below), in which case it should follow that arrangement.

Content areas are **compact — reserve most of the page for art** (aim for content ≲ 40% of
the sheet, with a big open hero region up top).

| Role | Relative size | Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| *(hero art)* | **Biggest — the top ~⅓, left blank of green** | Yes | The illustration; not a content area. |
| `event-list` | Largest *content* area, but compact | Yes | Stack of events; each row is just a title + short teaser. |
| `title` / masthead | Small strip | No | Standing header ("League Events" / the month). |
| `org-statement` | Small | Yes | Who + where the League is (free coding/robotics, grades 3–12, San Diego). |
| `qr` | Small **square** | Yes | QR code; short URL added beneath in CSS. |
| `nonprofit` | Thin strip | Yes | 501(c)(3) / nonprofit line, small. |
| `logo` | Small, a corner | No | Logo lock-up. |

## Zone Maps (concrete example layouts to hand the generator)
Two example arrangements live beside this file and can be passed to the model as a reference
image (image-to-image / layout guide). They show the exact green content rectangles on the
square master; the generator matches them and paints the chosen style around them.

- `zone-maps/peachjar-multi-event-A.svg` — list-dominant, QR bottom-right.
- `zone-maps/peachjar-multi-event-B.svg` — QR top-right, org statement as a full-width footer.

Regenerate or edit them with `zone-maps/generate_zone_maps.py` (it validates that every zone
fits inside the 8.5×11 crop and that squares stay square). The **Sidecar JSON** below is the
machine-readable twin of arrangement A.

## Style Integration
Style-agnostic — layer any League style on top; the border/background changes, the zone
map does not.

- **Comic book:** the whole sheet reads as a Golden-Age comic **page** — a bold inked
  panel frame around the edge, cream/newsprint field behind the list, small starburst
  furniture in the corners. The blank green rectangles are the "panels" the letterer will
  fill.
- **Technical blueprint:** the sheet is a **drafting sheet** — white border frame on deep
  blue paper, a title block in the lower-right, faint drafting grid, leader-line ticks
  pointing at each content area. The green rectangles read as blank annotation fields.
- **8-bit video game:** the sheet is an **NES menu / level-select screen** — tiled pixel
  border, a HUD bar top and bottom, dithered background. The green rectangles are the blank
  menu slots. (If the pixel scene uses greens, switch the key to magenta `#FF00FF`.)

## Template Mode (what the model actually draws)
> Generate a **square** flyer in [STYLE]. Render only the decorative frame, background, and
> style furniture. Leave every content area as a **flat, solid `#00FF00` (green-screen
> green) rectangle** with straight, axis-aligned edges and perfectly uniform color — no
> text, no placeholder copy, no drawn QR code, no gradient, border, shadow, or dots inside
> them. **The artwork is the hero: keep the top third or so an open illustration and hold all
> content areas to the lower portion — content should be well under half the sheet.** Include
> these compact content areas, all blank green, sized *relative* to each other; place them to
> suit the composition **or match the attached zone-map exactly if one is provided**:
> — a **compact rectangle** (the largest of the content areas, but not dominating the page)
>   for the event list — it holds only titles + short teasers;
> — a small, shallow **masthead strip** for the header;
> — a **small rectangle** for the organization/location statement;
> — a **small square** for a QR code;
> — a **thin strip** for the nonprofit line.
> Nothing in the artwork outside these rectangles may use `#00FF00`. Keep all rectangles
